message:
fix(ft-conformance): separate stdout/stderr overflow signals in legacy-oracle child wait

run_legacy_oracle_script_with_timeout was sharing a single
overflow_flag AtomicBool between the stdout-reader thread, the
stderr-reader thread, and wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit. That created
two unwanted couplings:

- A stderr overflow would race-poison the stdout reader's view of
  "I'm fine", and vice versa, so the overflow-message rendered to
  the caller couldn't say which stream actually overflowed —
  important for debugging an oracle script that legitimately
  produces lots of structured stdout but zero stderr.
- The wait loop terminated the child as soon as either flag fired,
  but only one error message ("legacy oracle exceeds max bytes")
  surfaced regardless of which stream tripped. That made overflow
  reports useless for triage when both streams ran near their
  caps.

Split the flag into stdout_overflow and stderr_overflow:

- Each reader thread gets its own Arc<AtomicBool>; the reader
  function still receives a single &AtomicBool but now it's the
  one specific to its stream.
- wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit takes both refs and ORs them in the
  loop body for the kill-on-overflow decision (so semantics around
  "kill when either stream overflows" are preserved exactly).
- After the wait returns, the caller checks each flag separately
  and emits one of two distinct error messages naming the
  offending stream and its specific cap (MAX_LEGACY_ORACLE_STDOUT_BYTES
  vs MAX_LEGACY_ORACLE_STDERR_BYTES). When both overflow, the
  stdout error wins by check order — fine because overflowed
  stdout is the more common cause and the underlying
  validate_legacy_oracle_stream_bounds will still catch the
  stderr overflow on the next run.
- The kill-and-reap path inside wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit no
  longer has to reverse-engineer which stream overflowed.

Test update:
- The wait_for_legacy_oracle_exit timeout test now constructs both
  flags and threads them through the new signature; assertions on
  the timeout-error string are unchanged.

Cargo.lock: catch-up resolution recording the previously-merged
addition of `insta = 1.47.2` to ft-serialize's [dev-dependencies]
(landed in 5de169f). No version bumps elsewhere.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
